sections.title: Attorney-General's consent required for prosecution of foreign public officials
sections.summary: This section requires the Attorney-General's consent for prosecution if alleged conduct occurs wholly within a foreign public official's jurisdiction. The consent is necessary for any prosecution actions related to foreign public officials. This applies to specific offences under Division 70 of the Act.

requirements.text: Attorney-General's consent required for prosecution if alleged conduct occurs wholly in a foreign public official's jurisdiction

sections.source_quote: 16.1 Attorney-General's consent required for prosecution if alleged conduct occurs wholly in a foreign public official's jurisdiction

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Conspiracy to commit offences punishable by imprisonment or fine
sections.summary: This section defines conspiracy as an agreement between two or more persons to commit an offence punishable by imprisonment exceeding 12 months or a fine of 200 penalty units. It specifies that guilt requires an agreement, shared intent to commit the offence, and an overt act. The section also outlines exceptions where conspiracy can still be proven despite impossible offences, involvement of non-criminally responsible persons, or acquittals of other parties.
sections.dtia_summary: Conspiracy provisions require explicit agreement and intent for cross-border data transfers involving multiple parties, impacting DTIA assessments for international collaboration or joint data processing activities.

requirements.text: A person who conspires with another person to commit an offence punishable by imprisonment for more than 12 months or by a fine of 200 penalty units or more commits the offence of conspiracy to commit that offence
The person must have entered into an agreement with one or more other persons
The person and at least one other party to the agreement must have intended that an offence would be committed pursuant to the agreement
The person or at least one other party to the agreement must have committed an overt act pursuant to the agreement
A person may be found guilty of conspiracy even if committing the offence is impossible
A person may be found guilty of conspiracy even if the only other party to the agreement is a body corporate
A person may be found guilty of conspiracy even if each other party to the agreement is a person who is not criminally responsible
A person may be found guilty of conspiracy even if each other party to the agreement is a person for whose benefit or protection the offence exists
A person may be found guilty of conspiracy even if all other parties to the agreement have been acquitted of the conspiracy
A person cannot be found guilty of conspiracy if all other parties to the agreement have been acquitted of the conspiracy and a finding of guilt would be inconsistent with their acquittal
A person cannot be found guilty of conspiracy if he or she is a person for whose benefit or protection the offence exists
A person cannot be found guilty of conspiracy if, before the commission of an overt act, the person withdrew from the agreement and took all reasonable steps to prevent the commission of the offence

sections.source_quote: A person who conspires with another person to commit an offence punishable by imprisonment for more than 12 months, or by a fine of 200 penalty units or more, commits the offence of conspiracy to commit that offence and is punishable as if the offence to which the conspiracy relates had been committed.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Definition of Australia for legal purposes
sections.summary: This section defines the term 'Australia' for the purposes of applying the Part to offences. It specifies that Australia has the same geographical meaning as in the provision creating the primary offence. For ancillary offences, it uses the same geographical meaning as in the primary offence to which the ancillary offence relates. If a provision extends to an external Territory, Australia includes that Territory. The section explicitly excludes the meanings of 'Australian aircraft', 'Australian citizen', and 'Australian ship'.

requirements.text: Australia has the same geographical meaning as in the provision creating the primary offence
Australia has the same geographical meaning as in the provision creating the primary offence to which the ancillary offence relates
If a provision extends to an external Territory, Australia includes that Territory

sections.source_quote: Section 16.3 (1) For the purposes of the application of this Part to a particular primary offence, Australia has the same meaning it would have if it were used in a geographical sense in the provision creating the primary offence.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: UN personnel protection offenses
sections.summary: This section defines offenses against UN personnel including unlawful detention, property damage, threats, and aggravated offenses. It specifies that a person commits an offense if they detain another without consent, cause damage to UN property, threaten to commit offenses under specified sections, or commit aggravated offenses during systematic infliction of pain, use of weapons, or abuse of authority. The offenses carry penalties ranging from 3 to 10 years imprisonment depending on the severity.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes specific offenses related to UN personnel that could impact data transfers involving UN personnel, particularly regarding security and jurisdictional requirements for cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: The person takes or detains another person without that other person's consent
The detention is not a UN enforcement action
The person's conduct causes damage to official premises, private accommodation or means of transportation
The property is occupied or used by a UN or associated person
The conduct gives rise to a danger of serious harm to a person
The UN or associated person is engaged in a UN operation
The person intends to cause damage to the property
The person is reckless as to the danger to the person
The person threatens to commit an offence under sections 71.2 to 71.11
The person intends to compel another person to act by making the threat
The offence was committed during deliberate and systematic infliction of severe pain
The offence was committed by use or threatened use of an offensive weapon
The offence was committed against a person in abuse of authority
The prosecution must prove the defendant intended to commit or was reckless as to committing the aggravated matters

sections.source_quote: Section 71.10: Unlawful detention of UN or associated person: (a) the person takes or detains another person without that other person's consent; and that is not a UN enforcement action.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: UN personnel definitions and operations
sections.summary: This section defines key terms including UN personnel, associated personnel, UN operations, and the conditions under which conduct causes death or harm. It specifies that UN personnel are those engaged by the UN in military, police, or civilian components of operations, and that UN operations must be for maintaining international peace or security, or declared by the Security Council as exceptional risk to personnel safety.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides definitions critical for understanding who qualifies as UN personnel and what constitutes a UN operation, which may impact data transfers involving UN personnel across borders.

requirements.text: Associated personnel means persons assigned by a government or intergovernmental organisation with UN agreement
Associated personnel means persons engaged by UN Secretary-General, specialised agencies or IAEA
Associated personnel means persons deployed by humanitarian NGOs under UN agreement
Convention means the Convention on the Safety of United Nations and Associated Personnel
UN operation means an operation established by UN in accordance with Charter for maintaining peace or security
UN operation means an operation declared by Security Council as exceptional risk to personnel safety
UN personnel means persons engaged by UN as members of military, police or civilian components
UN personnel means other officials or experts on mission of UN, specialised agencies or IAEA
A person's conduct causes death or harm if it substantially contributes to death or harm

sections.source_quote: Section 71.23: associated personnel means: (a) persons assigned by a government, or an intergovernmental organisation, with the agreement of the competent organ of the United Nations; or (b) persons engaged by the Secretary-General of the United Nations, a specialised agency or the International Atomic Energy Agency; or (c) persons deployed by a humanitarian non-governmental organisation or agency under an agreement with the Secretary-General of the United Nations, a specialised agency or the International Atomic Energy Agency; to carry out activities in support of the fulfilment of the mandate of a UN operation.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Cluster munitions: prohibited activities and authorized exceptions
sections.summary: This section prohibits individuals from using, developing, producing, acquiring, stockpiling, retaining, or transferring cluster munitions. It also establishes exceptions for authorized activities by the Australian Defence Force under the Defence Minister's written authorization for specific purposes like detection, training, counter-measures, or destruction. The section further outlines conditions for transfers to Convention parties and military cooperation with non-Convention countries without violating the prohibited activities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes specific conditions under which cluster munitions transfers are permitted without triggering prohibitions, relevant for assessing cross-border transfers of explosive materials in military contexts.

requirements.text: A person commits an offence if the person uses a cluster munition
A person commits an offence if the person develops, produces or otherwise acquires a cluster munition
A person commits an offence if the person stockpiles or retains a cluster munition
A person commits an offence if the person transfers a cluster munition to anyone

The Minister administering the Explosives Act 1961 may authorise specified members of the Australian Defence Force to acquire or retain specified cluster munitions for development, training, counter-measures, or destruction
The Minister may authorise acquisition or retention of cluster munitions for the development of detection, clearance or destruction techniques
The Minister may authorise acquisition or retention of cluster munitions for the development of counter-measures
The Minister may authorise acquisition or retention of cluster munitions for destruction of munitions
Section 72.38 does not apply to transfers of cluster munitions to Convention parties for development, training, counter-measures, or destruction

sections.source_quote: Section 72.38(1) A person commits an offence if the person does any of the following with a cluster munition: (a) uses it; (b) develops, produces or otherwise acquires it; (c) stockpiles or retains it; (d) transfers it to anyone.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Treason and related offences definitions
sections.summary: This section defines treason and related offences, including acts that cause death or harm to sovereigns, governors-general, or prime ministers, levying war against the Commonwealth, and instigating armed invasions. It specifies that assisting an enemy engaged in armed conflict involving the Commonwealth constitutes an offence, with strict liability for certain elements and absolute liability for others. The section also outlines conditions under which such conduct is not criminal, such as humanitarian aid.

requirements.text: A person commits an offence if they receive or assist another person who has committed an offence against this Subdivision with the intention of allowing them to escape punishment or apprehension

A person commits an offence if they know another person intends to commit an offence against this Subdivision but do not inform a constable within a reasonable time or use other reasonable endeavours to prevent the commission of the offence
A person commits an offence if they are engaged in armed conflict involving the Commonwealth or Australian Defence Force with an enemy declared in a Proclamation under section 80.1AB
A person commits an offence if they intend that their conduct will materially assist the enemy to engage in armed conflict involving the Commonwealth or Australian Defence Force
A person commits an offence if their conduct materially assists the enemy to engage in armed conflict involving the Commonwealth or Australian Defence Force
A person commits an offence if they know they are an Australian citizen or resident of Australia at the time of the conduct
A person commits an offence if they know they have voluntarily put themselves under the protection of the Commonwealth
A person commits an offence if they are a body corporate incorporated by or under a law of the Commonwealth or a State or Territory

sections.source_quote: Section 80.1AAA: A person commits an offence if the person: (a) receives or assists another person who, to his or her knowledge, has committed an offence against this Subdivision (other than this subsection) with the intention of allowing him or her to escape punishment or apprehension; or (b) knowing that another person intends to commit an offence against this Subdivision (other than this subsection), does not inform a constable of it within a reasonable time or use other reasonable endeavours to prevent the commission of the offence.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Public display of prohibited symbols and Nazi salutes
sections.summary: This section defines prohibited Nazi symbols, prohibited organisation symbols, and the meaning of displaying things in public places. It specifies that a prohibited Nazi symbol includes the Nazi hakenkreuz, the Nazi double-sig rune, and things that resemble these symbols. A prohibited organisation symbol relates to terrorist organisations, prohibited hate groups, or state sponsors of terrorism. The section also defines what constitutes displaying a thing in a public place, including digital platforms.

requirements.text: A prohibited Nazi symbol includes the Nazi hakenkreuz
A prohibited Nazi symbol includes the Nazi double-sig rune
A prohibited Nazi symbol includes something that so nearly resembles a thing to which paragraph (a) or (b) applies that it is likely to be confused with or mistaken for that thing
A prohibited organisation symbol includes a symbol that an organisation that is a terrorist organisation or prohibited hate group uses to identify the organisation
A prohibited organisation symbol includes something that so nearly resembles a symbol to which paragraph (a) applies that it is likely to be confused with or mistaken for that symbol
A prohibited organisation symbol includes a symbol that a state sponsor of terrorism uses to identify the state sponsor of terrorism
A prohibited organisation symbol includes something that so nearly resembles a symbol to which paragraph (c) applies that it is likely to be confused with or mistaken for that symbol
A thing is displayed in a public place if it is capable of being seen by a member of the public who is in a public place
A thing is also displayed in a public place if it is included in a document available to the public or a section of the public
A thing is also displayed in a public place if it is included in a film, video or television program available to the public or a section of the public
A thing is also displayed in a public place if it is included in a document, film, video or television program available to the public or a section of the public by means of the internet

sections.source_quote: Section 80.2F: A thing is displayed in a public place if it is capable of being seen by a member of the public who is in a public place (whether or not the thing is actually so seen by a member of the public).

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Police directions for ceasing display of prohibited symbols
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions under which police officers may direct individuals to cease displaying prohibited symbols in public places. The direction must specify a reasonable time for cessation, and may be given orally or in writing to persons suspected of causing the display or being owners/occupiers of premises where the symbol is displayed. The direction applies to specific categories of persons and can be delivered via physical placement on premises or vehicles.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes requirements for police to issue directions to stop prohibited symbols in public, relevant for cross-border data transfers involving symbols that could be part of international communications or displays.

requirements.text: Police direction must specify reasonable time for prohibited symbol cessation in public
Direction may be given orally or in writing to person suspected of causing symbol display
Direction may be given to owner/occupier of premises where symbol is displayed
Direction may be given to owner/occupant of aircraft, vehicle or vessel where symbol is displayed
Written direction may be left on premises where symbol is displayed
Written direction may be placed on aircraft, vehicle or vessel where symbol is displayed

sections.source_quote: Section 80.2L(1): A police officer may give a direction to cause a prohibited symbol to cease to be displayed in a public place under subsection 80.2K(1) only in accordance with subsection (2), (3) or (4) of this section.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Defence for acts done in good faith
sections.summary: This section provides a defence for individuals who act in good faith to correct errors in government, laws, or policies, or to address matters causing ill-will between groups. The defence applies to actions taken with the intention of assisting parties in armed conflict, or for purposes of public interest such as academic, artistic, scientific work, or news reporting.
sections.dtia_summary: This section may affect data transfer impact assessments when individuals engage in good faith activities that could involve cross-border data sharing or international communications.

requirements.text: Person may be defended if they try in good faith to show others are mistaken in counsels, policies or actions
Person may be defended if they point out errors in government, constitution or legislation in good faith
Person may be defended if they urge others to attempt lawful change to matters established by law
Person may be defended if they point out matters producing ill-will between groups to remove them
Person may be defended if they engage in good faith industrial dispute activities
Person may be defended if they publish good faith reports on matters of public interest

sections.source_quote: Section 80.3(1): Subdivisions B and C (other than sections 80.2A, 80.2B, 80.2BA, 80.2BB, 80.2BC, 80.2BD and 80.2BE), and sections 83.1 and 83.4, do not apply to a person who tries in good faith to show any of the following persons are mistaken in any of his or her counsels, policies or actions...

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: National security vulnerabilities and reckless conduct
sections.summary: This section defines when conduct becomes vulnerable to misuse or impairment, accessible by unauthorized persons, and part of public infrastructure, with reckless disregard for national security consequences. It specifies that reckless conduct causing such vulnerabilities in public infrastructure constitutes an offence against the Division. The section also outlines alternative verdicts and requires Attorney-General consent for prosecutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which technical vulnerabilities in public infrastructure could be considered national security threats, requiring careful assessment of data handling practices to avoid reckless conduct that impacts national security.

requirements.text: Conduct must result in an article or software becoming vulnerable to misuse or impairment
Conduct must result in an article or software being accessible or modifiable by unauthorized persons
Article or software must be part of public infrastructure
Conduct must be reckless regarding prejudice to Australia's national security

sections.source_quote: Section 82.9 (b) the conduct has the result that an article or thing, or software, becomes vulnerable: (i) to misuse or impairment; or (ii) to being accessed or modified by a person not entitled to access or modify it; and (c) the article or thing, or software, is or is part of public infrastructure; and (d) the person engages in the conduct reckless as to whether prejudice to Australia's national security will occur

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Defences for national security offences
sections.summary: This section outlines defences for national security offences, requiring the person to be a public official acting in good faith, with reasonable conduct within lawful authority. It also includes specific defences for foreign work authorizations and humanitarian purposes, which may exempt certain activities from liability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides defences that could apply to data handling activities involving foreign entities, particularly where activities are authorized under specific frameworks like the Defence Act 1903 or humanitarian purposes.

requirements.text: Person must be a public official at the time of the offence
Person must engage in conduct in good faith while performing duties
Conduct must be reasonable for the purpose of performing duties
Person must be an owner or operator of public infrastructure
Conduct must be within lawful authority of the owner or operator

sections.source_quote: Section 82.10 (a) the person is, at the time of the offence, a public official; and (b) the person engaged in the conduct in good faith in the course of performing duties as a public official; and (c) the conduct is reasonable in the circumstances for the purpose of performing those duties.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Security classification definitions and application
sections.summary: This section defines security classifications for information that could cause serious or exceptionally grave damage to national interest if disclosed improperly. It specifies that classifications include secret (serious damage) and top secret (exceptionally grave damage) levels, with equivalent classifications prescribed by regulations. The section also establishes strict liability for applying classifications according to the Commonwealth's policy framework and for regulations that align with these classifications.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines security classifications that could impact data transfer assessments when handling sensitive information across borders, requiring adherence to specific classification frameworks and policy frameworks for cross-border data transfers involving national security.

requirements.text: Information classified as secret if disclosed improperly could cause serious damage to national interest
Information classified as top secret if disclosed improperly could cause exceptionally grave damage to national interest
Regulations may prescribe equivalent security classifications
Classifications must be applied according to Commonwealth policy framework
Regulations must align with security classification policy framework

sections.source_quote: Section 90.6: for a classification of secret—that, if disclosed in an unauthorised manner, could be expected to cause serious damage to the national interest, organisations or individuals; or for a classification of top secret—that, if disclosed in an unauthorised manner, could be expected to cause exceptionally grave damage to the national interest

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Foreign interference offences and defences
sections.summary: This section outlines offences related to foreign interference, including intentional or reckless interference with political processes, democratic rights, intelligence activities, and national security. It specifies that interference must involve deception, threats, or demands with menaces, and includes defences such as prior publication or reasonable belief in non-prejudice to national security. The section also covers offences of preparing for such interference and geographical jurisdiction.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines foreign interference offences that could impact data transfers involving foreign entities, requiring assessments of whether data transfers constitute interference with national security or democratic processes across borders.

requirements.text: Person must engage in conduct on behalf of foreign principal
Conduct must influence political or governmental processes
Conduct must influence exercise of Australian democratic rights
Conduct must support foreign intelligence activities
Conduct must prejudice Australia's national security
Conduct must involve deception
Conduct must involve threats to cause serious harm
Conduct must involve demands with menaces
Person must conceal from target the circumstances of conduct

sections.source_quote: Section 92.2: (i) influence a political or governmental process of the [target]; (ii) influence the exercise (whether or not in Australia) of an Australian democratic or political right or duty; (iii) support intelligence activities of a foreign principal; (iv) prejudice Australia's national security

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Hearing in camera for national security
sections.summary: This section allows federal courts, territorial courts, or courts exercising federal jurisdiction to exclude the public from hearings or prohibit reporting of proceedings if national security interests are at stake. It also permits court-ordered restrictions on accessing sensitive documents without approval. Violating these orders constitutes an offence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for court-ordered restrictions on public access and document disclosure during proceedings involving national security, which may impact data transfer if sensitive information is shared across borders.

requirements.text: Judge may exclude public from hearing if national security interests require it
Judge may prohibit publication of proceedings if national security interests require it
Judge may order restrictions on document access without court approval

sections.source_quote: At any time before or during the hearing, the judge or magistrate, or other person presiding or competent to preside over the proceedings, may, if satisfied that it is in the interests of Australia's national security: order that some or all of the members of the public be excluded during the whole or a part of the hearing; or order that no report of the whole or a specified part of, or relating to, the application or proceedings be published; or make such order and give such directions as he or she thinks necessary for ensuring that no person, without the approval of the court, has access to any affidavit, exhibit, information or other document used in the application or the proceedings that is on the file in the court or in the records of the court.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions for terrorism-related legal terms
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in terrorism-related legislation, including 'terrorist act', 'detained in custody', 'referring State', and 'constitutional corporation'. These definitions establish the legal framework for applying anti-terrorism laws across Australian jurisdictions and international contexts.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions clarify how terrorism-related data and activities are treated across borders, particularly for cross-jurisdictional data transfers involving security intelligence.

requirements.text: Terrorist act means action or threat causing serious harm, damage, death, or endangering life
Detained in custody means detention under Commonwealth, State, or Territory law
Referring State means State that has referred matters to Commonwealth Parliament
Constitutional corporation means entity covered by Constitution paragraph 51(xx)

sections.source_quote: Terrorist act means an action or threat of action where: (a) the action falls within subsection (2) and does not fall within (b) the action is done or the threat is made with the intention of advancing a political, religious or ideological cause; and (c) the action causes serious harm that is physical harm to a person; or causes serious damage to property; or causes a person's death; or endangers a person's life, other than the life of the person; or creates a serious risk to the health or safety of the public or a section of the public; or affects an electronic system including information systems, telecommunications systems, financial systems, essential government services, public utilities, or transport systems.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Cross-border transfer of personal data: adequacy and safeguards
sections.summary: This section establishes conditions for cross-border data transfers between Australia and other countries, among States, or within Territories. It specifies that transfers must not disrupt banking, insurance, or international law obligations. The section also ensures concurrent operation of State and Territory laws without conflict, allowing for regulatory adjustments to maintain consistency.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires data transfers to avoid disrupting critical financial services and international legal obligations, ensuring cross-border transfers do not conflict with State/Territory laws. It mandates regulatory adjustments to maintain consistency in data handling across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: Transfers between Australia and places outside Australia
Transfers among the States
Transfers within a Territory between a State and a Territory or between 2 Territories
Transfers must not disrupt banking (other than State banking not extending beyond the limits of the State concerned)
Transfers must not disrupt insurance (other than State insurance not extending beyond the limits of the State concerned)
Transfers must not be actions in relation to which the Commonwealth is obliged to create an offence under international law
Transfers must not be threats in relation to which the Commonwealth is obliged to create an offence under international law
Subsections (2) and (3) apply to a State that is not a referring State at a particular time

sections.source_quote: Section 100.5 (i) between Australia and places outside Australia; or (ii) among the States; or (iii) within a Territory, between a State and a Territory or between 2 Territories; or (k) the action disrupts, or if carried out would disrupt: banking (other than State banking not extending beyond the limits of the State concerned); or insurance (other than State insurance not extending beyond the limits of the State concerned); or the action is, or if carried out would be, an action in relation to which the Commonwealth is obliged to create an offence under international law; or the threat is one in relation to which the Commonwealth is obliged to create an offence under international law.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Conditions for interim control orders in national security cases
sections.summary: This section details the conditions that must be imposed on individuals by an interim control order, including restrictions on movement, residence, communications, and electronic monitoring. The court must ensure these conditions are reasonably necessary and appropriate for protecting the public from terrorist acts or hostile activities abroad. The conditions must also consider the person's best interests, particularly for those aged 14-17, and their financial circumstances.
sections.dtia_summary: These conditions impose strict restrictions on individuals' movements, communications, and digital activities for national security purposes, requiring detailed monitoring and compliance mechanisms that could impact data transfers and cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: The court must ensure conditions are reasonably necessary and appropriate for protecting the public from terrorist acts or hostile activities abroad
The court must consider the person's best interests, particularly for those aged 14-17
The court must assess the impact of conditions on the person's financial and personal circumstances
Conditions may restrict the person from being present at specified areas or places
Conditions may require the person to reside at specified premises without changing residence without permission
Conditions may require the person to remain at specified premises between specified times each day
Conditions may prohibit the person from leaving Australia or their State/Territory
Conditions may require the person to provide their passport to a specified authority
Conditions may prohibit the person from changing their name or using names not specified in the order
Conditions may prohibit the person from applying for Australian travel documents or foreign travel documents
Conditions may prohibit the person from communicating with specified individuals or classes of individuals
Conditions may prohibit the person from accessing specified forms of telecommunication or technology
Conditions may prohibit the person from possessing or using specified articles or substances
Conditions may prohibit the person from engaging in specified work or activities
Conditions may require the person to attend treatment, rehabilitation or intervention programs
Conditions may require the person to provide specified information to a specified authority within a specified period
Conditions may require the person to comply with reasonable directions from a specified authority
Conditions may require the person to submit to testing by a specified authority
Conditions may require the person to allow themselves to be photographed by a specified authority
Conditions may require the person to allow fingerprints to be taken by a specified authority
Conditions may require the person to wear a monitoring device at all times
Conditions may require the person to be available to answer calls from a specified authority
Conditions may require the person to allow visits from specified authorities at any time
Conditions may require the person to provide a schedule of proposed movements for a specified period
Conditions may require the person to allow police to search their residence or premises
Conditions may require the person to facilitate access to electronic equipment for police searches
The person must not remain at specified premises for more than 12 hours within any 24 hours

sections.source_quote: Section 104.5A(1) states that conditions may relate to any of the following: [list of conditions]. Section 104.5A(2) specifies monitoring conditions and requirements for electronic monitoring.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Urgent interim control orders: electronic requests and court procedures
sections.summary: This section outlines procedures for requesting urgent interim control orders via electronic means without prior AFP Minister's consent. Senior AFP members must provide specific information including reasons for urgency and suspected matters under section 104.2(2). The court must consider the request and may issue the order within the same form as standard procedures. If the AFP Minister's consent is not obtained initially, it must be secured within 8 hours. The request must include sworn or affirmed explanations and specific details about the urgency.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes time-sensitive procedures for urgent cross-border data transfers involving electronic requests for interim control orders, requiring specific notifications and time limits for ministerial consent. It impacts DTIAs through its strict timelines and electronic communication requirements for urgent orders.

requirements.text: Senior AFP member may request urgent interim control order via electronic means if considers it necessary due to urgent circumstances
Senior AFP member must suspect matters under subsection 104.2(2) on reasonable grounds
Request must include all requirements under paragraphs 104.3(b) to (e) for ordinary interim control orders
Request must include explanation of why making the order is urgent
Request must include copy of AFP Minister's consent if obtained before request
Information and explanation in request must be sworn or affirmed by member
Court must consider information and explanation in request
Court may complete same form of order as under sections 104.4 and 104.5
If AFP Minister's consent not obtained before request, must obtain within 8 hours

sections.source_quote: A senior AFP member may request, by telephone, fax, email or other electronic means, an issuing court to make an interim control order in relation to a person if: (a) the member considers it necessary to use such means because of urgent circumstances; and (b) the member suspects the matters mentioned in subsection 104.2(2) on reasonable grounds.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Confirmation and service of control orders with specific conditions
sections.summary: This section outlines the conditions under which a confirmed control order takes effect and the requirements for its service. It specifies that the order begins to be in force only when the person is released from custody into the community without an active supervision order, and that the confirmed order must not end more than 12 months after the interim order was made. For minors aged 14-17, the order must end within 3 months. The section also mandates that the order must include specific details such as the person's name, conditions, duration, and the right for the person's lawyer to obtain a copy.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes specific conditions for when a control order becomes effective and requires precise service procedures, including the need for personal service and notification to minors' guardians. These conditions impact the timing and scope of data transfers related to individuals under control orders.

requirements.text: The confirmed control order begins to be in force when the person is released from custody into the community without an active supervision order.
The confirmed control order must not end more than 12 months after the interim order was made.
For minors aged 14-17, the confirmed control order must not end more than 3 months after the interim order was made.
The confirmed control order must specify the person's name, conditions, duration, and the right for the person's lawyer to obtain a copy.
The AFP must serve the confirmed control order personally on the person.
The AFP must inform the person of their right to appeal, apply for revocation, and have the order varied by consent.
The AFP must ensure the person understands the information provided, considering their age, language skills, and mental capacity.
The AFP must take reasonable steps to serve a copy of the order on a parent or guardian for minors aged 14-17.

sections.source_quote: Section 104.16(5) states: 'The confirmed control order begins to be in force when all of the following conditions are met: (a) the relevant interim control order did not begin to be in force when it was served personally on the person because of subsection 104.5(1D); (b) either of the following events occurs: (i) the person is released from custody in a prison into the community; (ii) the extended supervision order or interim supervision order referred to in paragraph 104.5(1D)(b) ceases to be in force in relation to the person; (c) when the event occurs, the person is in the community without an extended supervision order or interim supervision order being in force in relation to the person.'

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Variation of control orders: personal service and information requirements
sections.summary: When a control order is varied, the AFP must personally serve the varied order on the person, inform them of additional conditions, explain the effects of those conditions, and ensure they understand the information provided. The AFP must also inform the person of their right to appeal and review, and their right to apply for revocation or variation of the order. These obligations apply to the AFP member acting under section 104.24.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific communication requirements for varied control orders, including personal service and clear explanation of conditions to ensure understanding. These requirements impact data transfer assessments by establishing how personal data must be communicated across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: AFP must serve varied order personally on person
AFP must inform person of additional conditions
AFP must explain effect of additional conditions
AFP must inform person of effects of sections 104.18, 104.21, 104.27
AFP must inform person of right to apply for revocation or variation
AFP must ensure person understands information provided

sections.source_quote: Section 104.26(1)(a) to (d): 'As soon as practicable after a control order is varied under section 104.24, an AFP member: (a) must serve the varied order personally on the person; (b) must inform the person that the order has been varied to impose additional conditions; (c) must inform the person of the following: (i) the effect of the additional conditions; (ii) the effect of sections 104.18, 104.21 and 104.27 (and section 104.5C if appropriate); (iii) that the person may have appeal and review rights in relation to the decision of the issuing court to vary the order; (iv) the person's right to apply under section 104.18 for an order revoking or varying the order; (v) the right of the person or one or more representatives of relation to an application to revoke or vary the order; and (d) must ensure that the person understands the information provided under paragraph (c) (taking into account the person's age, language skills, mental capacity and any other relevant factor).'

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Reporting requirements for control orders
sections.summary: The AFP must report to the Minister on the number of interim and confirmed control orders, variations, revocations, and void declarations made during the year. This includes specific reporting on control orders for people aged 14 to 17 years. The reporting requirements apply to the AFP Commissioner and cover the operation of control orders under this Division.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires detailed reporting on control orders, which impacts data transfer assessments by establishing how personal data is tracked and reported across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: AFP must report number of interim control orders made under sections 104.4, 104.7, 104.9
AFP must report number of control orders confirmed under section 104.14
AFP must report number of control orders declared void under section 104.14
AFP must report number of control orders revoked under sections 104.14 and 104.20
AFP must report number of control orders varied under sections 104.14, 104.20, 104.24
AFP must report particulars of complaints made to Commonwealth Ombudsman related to control orders
AFP must report information given under section 40SA of Australian Federal Police Act 1979 related to control orders
AFP must report number of monitoring warrants issued under Division 5 of Part IAAB of Crimes Act 1914
AFP must report number of monitoring warrants executed
AFP must report the Ombudsman's report under subsection 3ZZUH(1) of Crimes Act 1914

sections.source_quote: Section 104.29: 'The Commissioner must cause: (a) the AFP Minister to be notified in writing if: (i) a control order is declared to be void under section 104.14; (ii) a control order is revoked under section 104.14 or 104.20; (iii) a control order is varied under section 104.14, 104.20 or 104.24; and (b) the AFP Minister to be given a copy of the varied order (if appropriate).'

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Preventative detention order conditions for reapplication
sections.summary: This section outlines conditions under which a new preventative detention order cannot be applied for based on assisting in preventing a different terrorist act within a specified period. It requires that the application or order must be based on information that became available only after the initial order was made. The section also specifies that if the person is taken into custody under the order, another order cannot be applied for on the basis of preserving evidence of the same terrorist act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes conditions on reapplying for preventative detention orders, requiring that new applications must be based on information available after the initial order was made. This affects cross-border data transfers involving individuals subject to such orders by limiting the scope of information used for reapplications.

requirements.text: An initial preventative detention order cannot be applied for on the basis of assisting in preventing a different terrorist act occurring within that period unless the application or order is based on information that became available to the issuing authority only after the initial order was made
An initial preventative detention order cannot be applied for on the basis of preserving evidence of the same terrorist act

sections.source_quote: Section 105.6(2): 'another initial preventative detention order cannot be applied for, or made, in relation to the person on the basis of assisting in preventing a different terrorist act occurring within that period unless the application, or the order, is based on information that became available to be put before an issuing authority only after the initial preventative detention order referred to in paragraph (a) was made.'

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Preventative detention order extensions and notifications
sections.summary: This section details the process for extending preventative detention orders, requiring that applications must be made in writing and specify the period for continued detention. It mandates that applicants notify the person of the proposed application and inform them that any material they provide will be shared with the issuing authority. The order must be in writing and the extension period cannot exceed 24 hours.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs the extension of preventative detention orders, requiring written applications and specific notifications to the person. It impacts data transfers involving individuals subject to such orders by limiting the duration of detention and requiring transparency in the application process.

requirements.text: An AFP member may apply for an extension of the period for which the order is in force in relation to a person
The extension must be made in writing
The extension period must end no later than 24 hours after the person is first taken into custody under the order
An AFP member must notify the person of the proposed application for a continued preventative detention order
An AFP member must inform the person that any material they provide will be put before the issuing authority

sections.source_quote: Section 105.10(1): 'If: (a) the person is taken into custody under the order; (b) the order is in force in relation to the person; an AFP member may apply to an issuing authority for initial preventative detention orders for an extension, or a further extension, of the period for which the order is to be in force in relation to the person.'

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Extension of continued preventative detention orders
sections.summary: An AFP member may apply to an issuing authority for an extension of a continued preventative detention order if the extension is reasonably necessary for the purpose of the original order. The application must include outcomes and particulars of previous extensions. The issuing authority may extend the order if it is satisfied the extension is reasonably necessary for the original purpose. The extension must end no later than 48 hours after the person is first taken into custody under the initial order.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires extensions of preventative detention orders to be time-bound (48 hours) and must be justified by the original purpose, impacting cross-border data transfers involving detained persons.

requirements.text: An AFP member may apply to an issuing authority for continued preventative detention orders for an extension of the period for which the continued preventative detention order is in force
The application must set out outcomes and particulars of all previous extensions
The issuing authority may extend the period if satisfied the extension is reasonably necessary for the original purpose
The extension must end no later than 48 hours after the person is first taken into custody under the initial order

sections.source_quote: Section 105.14(1) - If: (a) a continued preventative detention order is in force in an AFP member may apply to an issuing authority for continued preventative detention orders for an extension, or a further extension, of the period for which the continued preventative detention order is to be in force in relation to the person.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Prohibited contact orders for detained persons
sections.summary: Prohibited contact orders restrict a detained person's contact with specified individuals to prevent terrorist acts, serious harm, evidence preservation, or interference with investigations. The order requires the applicant to demonstrate reasonable necessity for the order. The issuing authority may refuse the order unless the applicant provides additional information. The order must be in writing and may be made orally in urgent circumstances with specific recording requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibited contact orders restrict communications with specific individuals to prevent terrorist activities, requiring justification and recording for data transfers involving detained persons.

requirements.text: An AFP member may apply for a prohibited contact order only if the order is reasonably necessary to avoid terrorist acts, prevent serious harm, preserve evidence, or prevent interference with investigations
The issuing authority may refuse the order unless the applicant provides additional information
The prohibited contact order must be in writing
If applied for orally in urgent circumstances, the issuing authority must ensure audio or audio-visual recording

sections.source_quote: Section 105.14A(4) - A person meets the requirements of this subsection if the person is satisfied that making the prohibited contact order is reasonably necessary: (a) to avoid a risk to action being taken to prevent a terrorist act occurring; or (b) to prevent serious harm to a person; or (c) to preserve evidence of, or relating to, a terrorist act; or (d) to prevent interference with the gathering of information about: (i) a terrorist act; or (ii) the preparation for, or the planning of, a terrorist act; or (e) to avoid a risk to: (i) the arrest of a person who is suspected of having committed an offence against this Part; or (ii) the taking into custody of a person in relation to whom a preventative detention order is in force, or in relation to whom a preventative detention order is likely to be made; or (iii) the service on a person of a control order or post-sentence order.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Police search and release during preventative detention
sections.summary: A police officer who takes a person into custody under a preventative detention order may conduct an ordinary search if they suspect the person is carrying evidence of a terrorist act or a seizable item. The officer must seize any such items found. If the person is being detained under a preventative order and a warrant under the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979 is in force, the officer must ensure the person is dealt with per the warrant. The officer may release the person from detention to comply with the warrant, but this does not extend the preventative detention order's validity. Senior AFP members must approve releases for non-AFP officers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines conditions for police searches and releases during preventative detention, which may impact data transfer if the person's data is involved in security investigations. The requirement for warrants and approvals could affect cross-border data handling in security contexts.

requirements.text: Police officer may conduct ordinary search if suspects person carries evidence of terrorist act or seizable item
Police officer must seize any items found during search
Police officer must ensure person is dealt with per warrant under Australian Security Intelligence Organisation Act 1979
Police officer may release person from detention to comply with warrant
Release does not extend preventative detention order's validity
Non-AFP officers must get senior AFP approval to release person

sections.source_quote: A police officer who takes a person into custody under a preventative detention order, or who is present when the person is taken into custody, may, if the police officer suspects on reasonable grounds that the person is carrying: (a) evidence of, or relating to, a terrorist act; or (b) a seizable item; conduct an ordinary search of the person at, or soon after, the time when the person is taken into custody, and seize any such thing found as a result of the search.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Detained person's rights to contact lawyer and ombudsman
sections.summary: A person detained under a preventative detention order has the right to contact the Commonwealth Ombudsman or a lawyer for specific purposes, including legal advice, court proceedings, complaints, and information under the Australian Federal Police Act 1979. The police officer must assist in choosing a lawyer if the person is unable to contact one due to prohibited contact orders or other barriers. Contact with lawyers must be monitored by police to ensure compliance with the order.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the rights of detained persons to contact lawyers and ombudsmen, which could impact data transfer if the person's data is shared with international entities. The monitoring requirement may affect data handling across borders.

requirements.text: Detained person entitled to contact Commonwealth Ombudsman for complaints
Detained person entitled to contact lawyer for legal advice on detention order
Detained person entitled to contact lawyer for court proceedings related to detention
Police officer must assist in choosing lawyer if prohibited contact order applies
Contact with lawyer must be monitored by police officer

sections.source_quote: The person being detained is entitled to contact: (a) the Commonwealth Ombudsman in accordance with subsections 7(3) to (5) of the Ombudsman Act 1976; or (b) a person referred to in subsection 40SA(1) of the Australian Federal Police Act 1979 in accordance with section 40SB of that Act.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Prohibited disclosures during detainee contact with third parties
sections.summary: This section prohibits specific disclosures by persons interacting with detained individuals under preventative detention orders. It defines offences for disclosing detainee's identity, detention status, detention period, or communications during contact with lawyers, parents/guardians, or interpreters. Disclosures must not occur without specific authorizations related to legal proceedings, ombudsman complaints, or AFP internal processes. The offences apply to the person making the disclosure and require the disclosure to occur while the detainee is being detained.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts data sharing during detainee contact with third parties by prohibiting disclosures of detainee's identity, detention status, or communications without specific authorizations related to legal proceedings or ombudsman complaints. Requires police to verify disclosures are for legitimate purposes before allowing sharing.

requirements.text: Police officer must arrange for interpreter services if reasonably practicable during detention
Police officer must arrange for interpreter services as soon as practicable if reasonably practicable
Person commits offence if disclosing detainee's identity, detention status, or detention period
Person commits offence if disclosing detainee's communications during contact
Disclosure must not occur while detainee is being detained
Disclosure must not be made for purposes not authorized under specific conditions

sections.source_quote: Section 105.41(1) - A person commits an offence if: (a) the subject is being detained under a preventative detention order; (b) the subject discloses to another person: (i) the fact that the subject is being detained; (ii) the period for which the subject is being detained; (c) the disclosure occurs while the subject is being detained; (d) the disclosure is not one that the subject is entitled to make under section 105.36, 105.37 or 105.39.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Post-sentence orders for terrorist offenders
sections.summary: This section establishes conditions under which post-sentence orders, including continuing detention orders and extended supervision orders, may be imposed on terrorist offenders. The orders apply to individuals convicted of serious offences, including international terrorist activities, serious Part 5.3 or 5.3A offences, or foreign incursions. The orders require specific preconditions, such as continuous detention in custody since conviction or the presence of a continuing detention order. The orders must be made by a court after satisfying certain conditions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the legal framework for post-sentence orders that may affect data handling for terrorist offenders in custody, including requirements for prison accommodation and treatment. It has implications for data storage and processing of sensitive information related to high-risk individuals.
sections.authority: Supreme Court of a State or Territory

requirements.text: A post-sentence order may be made under section 105A.7 or 105A.7A in relation to a person if the person has been convicted of a serious Part 5.3 or 5.3A offence
A post-sentence order may be made in relation to a person if the person is detained in custody in a prison serving a sentence of imprisonment for an offence referred to in paragraph 105A.3(1)(a)
A post-sentence order may be made in relation to a person if a continuing detention order or interim detention order is in force in relation to the person

A post-sentence order may be made in relation to a person if the person has been continuously detained in custody in a prison since being convicted of the offence referred to in paragraph 105A.3(1)(a)

A post-sentence order may be made in relation to a person if the person served a sentence of imprisonment for an offence referred to in paragraph 105A.3(1)(a) and the AFP Minister's consent to a request for an interim control order was sought under section 104.2
A continuing detention order commits the person to detention in a prison for the period the order is in force
An extended supervision order imposes conditions contravention of which is an offence

sections.source_quote: Section 105A.3(1) states: A post-sentence order may be made under section 105A.7 or 105A.7A in relation to a person if the person has been convicted of: (i) an offence against Subdivision A of Division 72 (international terrorist activities using explosive or lethal devices); or (ii) a serious Part 5.3 or 5.3A offence; or (iii) an offence against Part 5.5 (foreign incursions and recruitment)

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Terrorist offender post-sentence order assessment requirements
sections.summary: This section requires courts to conduct assessments for terrorist offenders applying for post-sentence orders. The assessment must include risk evaluations, offender behavior patterns, rehabilitation efforts, and compliance history. Courts must consider expert reports, rehabilitation participation, prior convictions, and sentencing court views when determining whether to impose continuing detention or extended supervision orders. The assessment process ensures ongoing monitoring of high-risk individuals through specific conditions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes detailed requirements for assessing terrorist offenders before imposing post-sentence orders, including risk evaluations and behavioral monitoring. These conditions directly impact data transfer considerations for law enforcement agencies handling sensitive offender information across jurisdictions.
sections.authority: Supreme Court of a State or Territory

requirements.text: The Court must hold a preliminary hearing within 28 days after application to determine if relevant experts should be appointed
The relevant expert must assess the risk of the offender committing serious Part 5.3 or 5.3A offences
The expert must provide a report to the Court, AFP Minister, and offender
The offender must attend the assessment
The assessment may not be admissible in criminal proceedings except for sentencing related to Division 104 offences
The assessment may not be admissible in civil proceedings except for Division 104 matters
The Court must consider expert reports and offender participation in assessments
The Court must consider the offender's history of prior convictions
The Court must consider the sentencing court's views at the time of sentencing

sections.source_quote: The Court must hold a preliminary hearing to determine whether to appoint one or more relevant experts. The hearing must be held within 28 days after a copy of the application is given to the offender.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Removal of monitoring devices by authorized personnel
sections.summary: This section permits specified authorities, police officers, or corrective services officers to remove monitoring devices and related equipment from terrorist offenders under certain conditions. Removal may occur without prior authorization if the order ceases to be in force, the offender is detained in custody, or specific conditions are met. The offender must be informed of the action, its timing, and the possibility of consent before removal.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines conditions under which monitoring devices can be removed without authorization, relevant to data transfer impact assessments involving surveillance equipment across borders.

requirements.text: One or more specified authorities, police officers, or corrective services officers may remove the monitoring device
One or more police officers may remove any related monitoring equipment
Removal may occur without authorization if the order ceases to be in force
Removal may occur without authorization if the offender is detained in custody
Before removal, the offender must be informed of the action, timing, and possibility of consent
If consent is not given, reasonable force may be used to take the action

sections.source_quote: Section 105A.7E (d) one or more specified authorities, police officers or corrective services officers to remove the monitoring device; and (e) one or more police officers to remove any related monitoring equipment.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Variation of supervision orders by application
sections.summary: This section requires applications for variations of extended or interim supervision orders to be made to the Supreme Court. Applications must include specific documentation, such as copies of conditions, explanations for changes, and previous application records. The Court may vary the order if it is satisfied that the changes are necessary for community protection.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details the process for varying supervision orders, relevant to data transfer impact assessments involving third-party data handling and monitoring.

requirements.text: The AFP Minister or legal representative must apply to vary conditions if they are no longer reasonably necessary
The terrorist offender or legal representative may apply to vary conditions
Applications must be made to the Supreme Court where the offender resides
Applications must include copies of conditions and explanations for changes
The Court may vary the order if it is satisfied that conditions are reasonably necessary for community protection

sections.source_quote: Section 105A.9B (1A) If the AFP Minister is satisfied that a condition in an extended terrorist offender is no longer reasonably necessary, or reasonably appropriate and adapted, for the purpose of protecting the community from the unacceptable risk of the offender committing a serious Part 5.3 or 5.3A offence, the Minister or a legal representative of the Minister must apply to a Supreme Court of a State or Territory to vary, under section 105A.9C, the order by: (a) removing the condition; or (b) varying the condition.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Review process for terrorist offender post-sentence orders
sections.summary: This section outlines the procedure for reviewing post-sentence orders of terrorist offenders. The Court must determine if the order should be affirmed, revoked, or varied based on whether the order was reasonably necessary to prevent serious offences. The review involves specific parties, relevant experts, and requires the AFP Minister to provide evidence supporting the order's validity. The Court may also impose extended supervision orders if the original order is revoked.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a review mechanism for post-sentence orders of terrorist offenders, requiring courts to assess whether the order remains necessary for community protection. It impacts DTIAs by mandating specific review procedures and conditions for order validity.
sections.authority: Supreme Court of a State or Territory

requirements.text: The Court may affirm the order if it could have made the order under section 105A.7 for continuing detention orders
The Court may affirm the order if it could have made the order under section 105A.7A for extended supervision orders
The Court must consider making an extended supervision order if it does not affirm the continuing detention order
The AFP Minister must ensure reasonable inquiries are made to ascertain facts supporting the order's invalidity
The AFP Minister bears the onus of satisfying the Court of matters referred to in section 105A.7 or 105A.7A
The Court must vary the period specified in a post-sentence order if it is not reasonably necessary to prevent serious offences
The Court must vary or remove conditions if they are not reasonably necessary for community protection

sections.source_quote: The Court may affirm the order (including affirm the order with variations made under section 105A.12A) if, after having regard to the matters in section 105A.6B, the Court is satisfied that the Court: (a) for a continuing detention order—could have made the order under section 105A.7; or (b) for an extended supervision order—could have made the order under section 105A.7A, or could have made the order disregarding paragraph 105A.7A(1)(c).

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Police detention powers for terrorist offenders under supervision orders
sections.summary: This section grants police officers authority to detain terrorist offenders in non-prison custody to enforce continuing or interim detention orders. It specifies that such detention carries the same powers and obligations as arrest for an offence, with the definition of 'offence' depending on whether the officer is an AFP member or a member of another police force. The section also states that persons detained in non-prison custody are considered to be in the community for the purposes of this Part.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions under which police can detain terrorist offenders outside prisons, relevant to data transfers involving individuals subject to supervision orders.

requirements.text: Any police officer may take the offender into custody for the purpose of giving effect to the order
Any police officer may detain the offender for the purpose of giving effect to the order
A police officer taking or detaining an offender under this subsection has the same powers and obligations as if arresting or detaining for an offence
A person detained in non-prison custody is taken to be in the community for the purposes of this Part

sections.source_quote: Section 105A.18AA (3) If a continuing detention order or interim detention order is in force in relation to the offender at any time after the offender is released as mentioned in paragraph (1)(b): (a) any police officer may take the offender into custody; and (b) any police officer may detain the offender; for the purpose of giving effect to the order.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Transitional provisions for regulations
sections.summary: This section provides transitional provisions for regulations originally made for the purposes of paragraph (c) of the definition of terrorist organisation. It states that such regulations continue to have effect after commencement as if they had been made for paragraph (b) of the definition of terrorist organisation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines transitional provisions for regulations related to terrorist organisations, relevant to data transfers involving individuals subject to supervision orders.

requirements.text: Regulations made before commencement for the purposes of paragraph (c) of the definition of terrorist organisation continue to have effect after commencement as if made for paragraph (b) of the definition of terrorist organisation

sections.source_quote: Section 106.1 (1) If regulations were made before commencement for the purposes of paragraph (c) of the definition of terrorist organisation in subsection 102.1(1), as in force before commencement; and the regulations were in force immediately before commencement; the regulations have effect, after commencement, as if they had been made for the purposes of paragraph (b) of the definition of terrorist organisation in subsection 102.1(1), as in force after commencement.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Application of amended counter-terrorism provisions to control orders
sections.summary: This section specifies that certain amendments to counter-terrorism legislation do not affect existing regulations for the period before the amendment. It outlines when amended sections apply to interim control orders, preventative detention orders, and variations of control orders made after the commencement of the section, regardless of when the underlying conduct occurred. The provisions ensure continuity of orders and applications made before the amendment takes effect.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that amended counter-terrorism provisions apply to orders made after the commencement date, maintaining continuity for control orders and preventative detention orders regardless of when the underlying conduct occurred. This affects data transfer assessments involving terrorism-related data.

requirements.text: Amendments to section 102.1 do not affect continuity of regulations in force before commencement
Section 104.2 applies to interim control orders made after commencement where conduct occurs before or after commencement
Section 104.4 applies to orders made after commencement where conduct occurs before or after commencement
Sections 104.6 and 104.8 apply to requests made after commencement where conduct occurs before or after commencement
Section 104.23 applies to variations of control orders where interim control order is requested after commencement
Section 105.4 applies to applications for preventative detention orders made after commencement
Section 105.7 applies to initial preventative detention orders made after commencement
Section 105.8 applies to initial preventative detention orders made after commencement
Section 105.12 applies to continued preventative detention orders made after commencement regardless of initial order timing
Section 105.15 applies to prohibited contact orders made after commencement regardless of preventative detention order timing
Section 105.16 applies to prohibited contact orders made after commencement regardless of preventative detention order timing

sections.source_quote: Section 106.5(1) The amendments of section 102.1, made by Schedule 1 to the Counter-Terrorism Legislation Amendment (Foreign Fighters) Act 2014, do not affect the continuity of any regulations that are in force for the purposes of that section immediately before the

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions for state sponsors of terrorism
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the state sponsors of terrorism framework, including Australian permanent resident, foreign state entity, funds, members of an entity, and state sponsor of terrorism. It specifies that state sponsor of terrorism refers to entities specified by regulations, and provides criteria for entities to be considered as having advocated terrorist acts targeted at Australia.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions establish the legal framework for identifying state sponsors of terrorism, which is critical for data transfer assessments involving terrorism-related data flows and cross-border data processing.

requirements.text: Australian permanent resident means a person who is a permanent resident within the meaning of the Australian Citizenship Act 2007
Foreign state entity means any of the following entities: a country, an authority of the government of a foreign country, a government body, or an entity prescribed by regulations
Funds means credit
Member of an entity includes employees, contractors, individuals holding positions under foreign law, individuals in service, and authorized intermediaries
State sponsor of terrorism means an entity specified by regulations for the purposes of this definition

sections.source_quote: Section 110.1 Australian permanent resident means a person who is a permanent resident within the meaning of the Australian Citizenship Act 2007.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: State sponsor of terrorism activities and offences
sections.summary: This section defines offences related to state sponsors of terrorism, including providing training, possessing items, and collecting documents that facilitate terrorist acts. It specifies that a person commits an offence if they engage in activities that prepare for or plan terrorist acts, or assist in terrorist acts. The offences apply to both individuals and entities, with specific exceptions for lawful activities and certain defences.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for when data transfers involving state sponsors of terrorism could be prohibited, requiring assessments of whether activities constitute facilitation of terrorist acts. It impacts DTIAs by introducing restrictions on cross-border data flows related to terrorism.

requirements.text: A person commits an offence if they engage in activities that prepare for or plan terrorist acts
A person commits an offence if they assist in terrorist acts
A person commits an offence if they possess items connected with terrorist acts
A person commits an offence if they collect documents likely to facilitate terrorist acts
A person commits an offence if they provide or receive training connected with terrorist acts
A person commits an offence if they engage in activities that prepare for or plan state terrorist acts
A person commits an offence if they engage in activities that prepare for or plan specific state terrorist acts
A person commits an offence if they engage in activities that prepare for or plan more than one state terrorist act
A person commits an offence if they direct the activities of a state sponsor of terrorism
A person commits an offence if they are a member of a foreign state entity that is a state sponsor of terrorism
A person commits an offence if they provide support to a state sponsor of terrorism
A person commits an offence if they associate with a state sponsor of terrorism
A person commits an offence if they are reckless as to whether funds will be used for state terrorist acts
A person commits an offence if they provide funds to a state sponsor of terrorism
A person commits an offence if they collect funds for a state sponsor of terrorism

sections.source_quote: Section 111.6: Other acts done in preparation for, or planning, state terrorist acts

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Definition of prohibited hate groups and related terms
sections.summary: This section defines key terms including 'hate crime', 'prohibited hate group', and 'organisations'. It specifies that a hate crime involves conduct constituting offences under certain laws related to race or national/ethnic origin. A prohibited hate group is an organisation specified by regulations that engages in or advocates hate crimes. The definitions include conditions for when conduct constitutes a hate crime and the legal basis for specifying organisations as prohibited hate groups.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines terms critical for identifying prohibited hate groups, which may impact cross-border data transfers involving organisations engaged in hate-related activities.

requirements.text: A hate crime is conduct that would constitute an offence under Subdivision C of Division 80 (urging or threatening violence and offences against groups or members of groups) to the extent the targeted group is distinguished by race or national or ethnic origin
A hate crime is conduct that involves publicly inciting hatred because of race or national or ethnic origin and would cause a reasonable person to fear intimidation or violence
A prohibited hate group is an organisation specified by regulations for the purposes of this definition
The AFP Minister must be satisfied on reasonable grounds that the organisation has engaged in, prepared or planned to engage in, or assisted the engagement in conduct constituting a hate crime
The AFP Minister must be satisfied that specifying the organisation as a prohibited hate group is reasonably necessary to protect the Australian community against harm

sections.source_quote: Section 114A.2(1): A hate crime is conduct (whether engaged in before or after this section commences) that would constitute an offence under Subdivision C of Division 80 (urging or threatening violence and offences against groups or members of groups) to the extent the targeted group is distinguished by race or national or ethnic origin

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
