sections.title: Process for specifying organisations as prohibited hate groups
sections.summary: This section outlines the process for the AFP Minister to specify organisations as prohibited hate groups. It requires the AFP Minister to obtain the Attorney-General's written agreement and arrange for the Leader of the Opposition to be consulted before making a prohibited hate group regulation. The process includes receiving advice from the Director-General of Security and ensuring procedural fairness is not required.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies the procedural requirements for identifying organisations as prohibited hate groups, which may affect data transfers involving such organisations due to heightened security risks.

requirements.text: The AFP Minister must obtain the Attorney-General's written agreement before specifying an organisation as a prohibited hate group
The AFP Minister must arrange for the Leader of the Opposition to be consulted before making a prohibited hate group regulation
The AFP Minister may consider recommending that the Governor-General makes a prohibited hate group regulation only if they have received advice from the Director-General of Security

sections.source_quote: Section 114A.7(1): Before the Governor-General makes a prohibited hate group regulation specifying an organisation, the AFP Minister must obtain the Attorney-General's agreement in writing to the organisation being specified

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Murder and manslaughter of Australians abroad
sections.summary: This section defines offences for murder or manslaughter of Australian citizens or residents occurring outside Australia. It requires that the conduct causes death, involves intent or recklessness regarding death, and applies to conduct before or after October 1, 2002. The section specifies that offences committed before this date must have constituted an offence under foreign law at the time. Absolute liability applies to certain elements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes jurisdictional conditions for offences against Australians abroad, relevant to cross-border data transfers involving individuals who commit acts outside Australia that cause harm to Australians.

requirements.text: The person engages in conduct outside Australia
The conduct causes death of another person
The person intends to cause death or is reckless as to causing death
The conduct constitutes an offence under foreign law at the time (for conduct before 1 October 2002)

sections.source_quote: Section 115.1: (a) the person engages in conduct outside Australia (whether before or after 1 October 2002 or the commencement of this Code); and (b) the conduct causes the death of another person; and (c) the person is an Australian citizen or resident of Australia; and (d) the first-mentioned person intends to cause, or is reckless as to causing, the death of the Australian citizen or resident of Australia or any other person by the conduct; and (e) if the conduct was engaged in before 1 October 2002—at the time the conduct was engaged in, the conduct constituted an offence against a law of the foreign country, or the part of the foreign country, in which the conduct was engaged.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Foreign incursions and recruitment offences
sections.summary: This section defines offences related to hostile activities in foreign countries, including incursions, recruitment, and preparations for hostile activities. It specifies that a person engages in hostile activities if they intend to overthrow governments, engage in actions that would constitute serious offences, or cause harm to public officials. The section also outlines exceptions for humanitarian purposes, official duties, and military service.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes jurisdictional conditions for offences against Australians abroad, relevant to cross-border data transfers involving individuals who commit acts outside Australia that cause harm to Australians.

requirements.text: The person enters a foreign country with the intention of engaging in a hostile activity
The person engages in hostile activities in a foreign country
The person is not engaged in legitimate purposes such as humanitarian aid, official duties, or journalism

sections.source_quote: Section 119.1: (a) the person enters a foreign country with the intention of engaging in a hostile activity in that or any other foreign country; and (b) when the person enters the country, the person: (i) engages in conduct that would constitute a serious offence under Australian law; (ii) is not engaged in legitimate purposes such as humanitarian aid, official duties, or journalism.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Military training and recruitment for hostile activities
sections.summary: This section prohibits engaging in conduct that prepares or facilitates military training for offences under section 119.1, including allowing oneself to be present at meetings, providing goods/services to promote offences, or permitting buildings/vessels/aircraft to be used for such purposes. It also covers recruiting persons to join organisations engaged in hostile activities against foreign governments or armed forces. Absolute liability applies to certain conduct, and humanitarian activities are exempt.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes restrictions on cross-border military training and recruitment activities that could impact data transfers involving sensitive security information. It requires assessments of whether data transfers relate to prohibited activities.

requirements.text: Person must engage in conduct intending to prepare another to commit an offence against section 119.1
Person must allow military training to be provided to themselves
Person must permit meetings of persons intending to allow military training to be provided to themselves
Person must give money or goods to, or perform services for, another person to promote offences
Person must permit buildings, vessels or aircraft to be used for offences
Person must intend to commit or support offences against section 119.4
Person must recruit another person to join organisations engaged in hostile activities against foreign governments
Person must engage in conduct intending to facilitate recruitment for foreign armed forces

sections.source_quote: Section 119.4: Person must engage in conduct intending to prepare another to commit an offence against section 119.1

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Secrecy of information and definitions
sections.summary: This section defines key terms related to secrecy of information, including 'cause harm to Australia's interests', 'Commonwealth officer', 'domestic intelligence agency', 'foreign military organisation', 'inherently harmful information', and 'security classified information'. It specifies conditions under which communications with such information are prohibited and outlines the legal framework for handling sensitive information.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions establish the scope of information that requires special handling in cross-border data transfers, particularly sensitive security information that could impact national security or international relations.

requirements.text: Cause harm to Australia's interests means interfering with criminal investigations or defence operations
Commonwealth officer includes APS employees and Defence Force members
Domestic intelligence agency includes ASIO and Australian Security Intelligence Organisation
Inherently harmful information includes security classified information

sections.source_quote: Section 121.1: Cause harm to Australia's interests means to interfere with or prejudice the prevention, detection, investigation or prosecution of a criminal offence against a law of the Commonwealth

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Security classification and disclosure of information by Commonwealth officers
sections.summary: This section establishes conditions under which Commonwealth officers may be held liable for unauthorized disclosure of information. It specifies that disclosure is unlawful if the information was obtained by the officer through their Commonwealth role, the disclosure violates a duty not to disclose, and the disclosure causes or is likely to cause harm to Australia's interests. The section also outlines aggravating factors for enhanced penalties, including the handling of classified records, multiple records with security classifications, and the officer having a high-level security clearance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes restrictions on cross-border data transfers involving Commonwealth officers by requiring that disclosures of classified information do not harm national security. It mandates specific safeguards for handling sensitive information to prevent unauthorized access or disclosure.

requirements.text: The person must have made or obtained information by reason of being a Commonwealth officer
The person must be under a duty not to disclose the information
The person must not disclose information that causes harm to Australia's interests

sections.source_quote: Section 122.4: (b) the person made or obtained the information by reason of his or her being, or having been, a Commonwealth officer or otherwise engaged to perform work for a Commonwealth entity; and (c) the person is under a duty not to disclose the information; and (d) the duty arises under a law of the Commonwealth.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Defences for unauthorized disclosure of information
sections.summary: This section outlines defences that may prevent prosecution for unauthorized disclosure of information. It includes scenarios where information is communicated to integrity agencies, in accordance with public interest disclosure laws, for legal advice, news reporting, or to royal commissions. The defences require that the person reasonably believed the disclosure was in the public interest or authorized by law, and that the disclosure did not cause harm to Australia's interests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides defences that can be used when transferring sensitive information across borders, particularly for Commonwealth officers. It ensures that disclosures made for legitimate purposes, such as public interest or legal compliance, are not penalized, thereby facilitating lawful data transfers without compromising national security.

requirements.text: The person must reasonably believe the disclosure is in the public interest
The person must not engage in conduct that would be an offence under specific laws
The person must not have engaged in conduct that harms Australia's interests

sections.source_quote: Section 122.5: (3) It is a defence to a prosecution for an offence by a person against this Division that: (a) the person communicated, removed, held or otherwise dealt with the relevant information for the primary purpose of communicating it to a Royal Commission within the meaning of the Royal Commissions Act 1902 (whether or not as a result of a requirement); and (b) at that time, the person reasonably believed that the relevant information was relevant to the Royal Commission's inquiry.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions of key legal terms for property and property rights
sections.summary: This section defines critical legal terms including 'duty', 'gain', 'loss', 'obtaining', 'property', 'services', and 'supply'. It specifies that property encompasses real and personal property, money, intangible assets, and certain living creatures. The definitions establish the legal framework for determining ownership, possession, and the transfer of property rights within Commonwealth law. These definitions apply to all persons, including Commonwealth entities, and are foundational for subsequent property-related offences.

requirements.text: duty: in relation to a Commonwealth public official means any authority, duty, function or power conferred on the person as a Commonwealth public official or held by the person as such
gain: a gain in property, whether temporary or permanent, or a gain by way of the supply of services, including keeping what one has
loss: a loss in property, whether temporary or permanent, including not getting what one might get
obtaining: obtaining for another person or inducing a third person to do something that results in another person obtaining

property includes real property, personal property, money, a thing in action or other intangible property, electricity, and wild creatures that are tamed, ordinarily kept in captivity, or reduced into the possession of a person
services includes any rights, benefits, privileges or facilities but does not include rights or benefits being the supply of goods
supply includes in relation to goods: supply (including re-supply) by way of sale, exchange, lease, hire or hire-purchase; and in relation to services: provide, grant or confer

sections.source_quote: In this Chapter: duty: (a) in relation to a person who is a Commonwealth public official—means any authority, duty, function or power that: (i) is conferred on the person as a Commonwealth public official; or (ii) the person holds himself or herself out as having as a Commonwealth public official; and (b) in relation to a person who is a public official—means any authority, duty, function or power that: (i) is conferred on the person as a public official; or (ii) the person holds himself or herself out as having as a public official; gain means: (a) a gain in property, whether temporary or permanent; or (b) a gain by way of the supply of services; and includes keeping what one has.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Burglary and aggravated burglary offences with Commonwealth property
sections.summary: This section defines burglary as entering or remaining in a building as a trespasser with intent to commit an offence causing harm or property damage, where the offence is punishable by imprisonment for life or 5+ years and the building is owned/occupied by a Commonwealth entity. It specifies that aggravated burglary involves committing burglary with one or more others or with an offensive weapon. Absolute liability applies to the Commonwealth entity ownership element. The penalty is 13 years imprisonment for burglary and 17 years for aggravated burglary.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for Commonwealth property offences involving burglary that could impact data transfers if data is stored in Commonwealth-owned buildings or systems.

requirements.text: The person enters or remains in a building as a trespasser with intent to commit an offence causing harm to another person or damage to property
The offence is punishable by imprisonment for life or 5 years or more
The building is owned or occupied by a Commonwealth entity
The person commits an offence if they enter or remain in a building as a trespasser with intent to commit an offence causing harm or property damage
The offence is an offence against a law of the Commonwealth, a State or a Territory
The person commits aggravated burglary if they commit burglary with one or more others or with an offensive weapon
Absolute liability applies to the building being owned or occupied by a Commonwealth entity

sections.source_quote: Section 132.4(6): A person commits an offence if: (a) the person enters, or remains in, a building, as a trespasser, with intent to commit an offence in the building that involves causing harm to another person or damage to property; and (aa) the offence referred to in paragraph (a) is an offence against a law of the Commonwealth, a State or a Territory; and (b) the offence referred to in paragraph (a) is punishable by imprisonment for life or for a term of 5 years or more; and (c) the building is owned or occupied by a Commonwealth entity.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Obtaining property or financial advantage by deception
sections.summary: This section defines offences involving obtaining property or financial advantage through deception. It specifies that obtaining property means acquiring ownership, possession, or control, or enabling such acquisition. It covers cases where property is transferred via accounts, credit/debit transactions, or other financial mechanisms. The offences include theft, aggravated robbery, burglary, and other property-related offences. Absolute liability applies to certain elements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines deception-related offences that could impact data transfers if data is obtained through deceptive practices involving Commonwealth entities.

requirements.text: The person obtains property belonging to another with the intention of permanently depriving the other of the property
The person obtains property by deception
The person causes an amount to be transferred from an account held by another person to an account held by themselves
The person causes an amount to be transferred from an account held by another person to an account held by a third person
The person conspires with another person to dishonestly obtain a gain from a Commonwealth entity
The person conspires with another person to dishonestly cause a loss to a Commonwealth entity

sections.source_quote: Section 134.1(1): A person commits an offence if the person, by a deception, dishonestly obtains property belonging to another with the intention of permanently depriving the other of the property.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: False statements in Commonwealth applications
sections.summary: This section defines when making false or misleading statements in applications to Commonwealth entities constitutes an offence. It requires the statement to be made to a Commonwealth entity, in connection with an application for a licence, permit, authority, registration, or benefit, and the person must know the statement is false or misleading in a material particular. The offence includes both recklessness and absolute liability for certain elements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes restrictions on false statements in Commonwealth applications, relevant to DTIAs when transferring data to Commonwealth entities or applications requiring data accuracy.

requirements.text: The person makes a statement (whether orally, in a document or in any other way)
The person knows the statement is false or misleading in a material particular
The statement is made to a Commonwealth entity
The statement is made in connection with an application for a licence, permit or authority
The statement is made in connection with an application for registration
The statement is made in connection with an application or claim for a benefit

sections.source_quote: Section 136.1: (a) the person makes a statement (whether orally, in a document or in any other way); and (b) the person does so knowing that the statement: (ii) omits any matter or thing without which the statement is misleading in a material particular; (c) the statement is made in, or in connection with: (i) an application for a licence, permit or authority; or (ii) an application for registration; or (iii) an application or claim for a benefit; and (i) the statement is made to a Commonwealth entity

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: False Commonwealth documents
sections.summary: This section defines false Commonwealth documents as those made by or on behalf of Commonwealth entities or public officials without proper authority. It specifies that a false Commonwealth document must be created by a Commonwealth entity or public official who did not make it in that form or terms, or was authorized by someone who did not authorize its making.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines false Commonwealth documents, relevant to DTIAs when data is transferred to Commonwealth entities or processed by public officials.

requirements.text: A document is a false Commonwealth document if made by a Commonwealth entity, or a Commonwealth public official, who did not make it in that form
A document is a false Commonwealth document if made on the authority of a Commonwealth entity, or a Commonwealth public official, who did not authorize its making in that form
A document is a false Commonwealth document if made by a Commonwealth entity, or a Commonwealth public official, who did not make it in those terms
A document is a false Commonwealth document if made on the authority of a Commonwealth entity, or a Commonwealth public official, who did not authorize its making in those terms

sections.source_quote: Section 143.3: (1) For the purposes of this Part, a document is a false Commonwealth document if, and only if: (i) made by a Commonwealth entity, or a Commonwealth public official, who did not make it in that form; or (ii) made on the authority of a Commonwealth entity, or a Commonwealth public official, who did not authorise its making in that form; or (iii) made by a Commonwealth entity, or a Commonwealth public official, who did not make it in those terms; or (iv) made on the authority of a Commonwealth entity, or a Commonwealth public official, who did not authorise its making in those terms

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Falsification of Commonwealth documents and related offences
sections.summary: This section defines offences for dishonestly damaging, destroying, altering, concealing or falsifying Commonwealth documents. The documents must be kept, retained or issued for legal purposes, made or held by Commonwealth entities or officials, and the act must cause loss. Absolute liability applies to the loss element. The offences also include giving information derived from false or misleading documents to others with intent to gain or cause loss to a Commonwealth entity.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for when falsifying Commonwealth documents constitutes an offence, relevant to DTIAs involving Commonwealth entities or officials. It specifies that documents must be legally maintained by Commonwealth entities and that the act must cause loss.

requirements.text: The person dishonestly damages, destroys, alters, conceals or falsifies a document
The document is kept, retained or issued for the purposes of a law of the Commonwealth
The document is made by a Commonwealth entity or a person in the capacity of a Commonwealth public official
The document is held by a Commonwealth entity or a person in the capacity of a Commonwealth public official
The person does so with the intention of obtaining a gain from another person
The person does so with the intention of causing a loss to another person
The other person is a Commonwealth entity
The person dishonestly gives information to another person
The information was derived from a document that is false or misleading in a material particular
The document is kept, retained or issued for the purposes of a law of the Commonwealth
The document is made by a Commonwealth entity or a person in the capacity of a Commonwealth public official
The document is held by a Commonwealth entity or a person in the capacity of a Commonwealth public official
The person does so with the intention of obtaining a gain from another person
The person does so with the intention of causing a loss to another person
The other person is a Commonwealth entity

sections.source_quote: Section 145.4: (a) the person dishonestly damages, destroys, alters, conceals or falsifies a document; and (b) the document is: (i) kept, retained or issued for the purposes of a law of the Commonwealth; (ii) made by a Commonwealth entity or a person in the capacity of a Commonwealth public official; or (iii) held by a Commonwealth entity or a person in the capacity of a Commonwealth public official; and (ii) causing a loss.

sections.heading: 50 (cont.)
sections.title: Impersonation and obstruction of Commonwealth public officials
sections.summary: This section covers offences related to impersonating Commonwealth public officials, obstructing their functions, and making false representations about Commonwealth bodies. It defines specific conduct that constitutes obstruction, including knowing another person is a public official and obstructing them in performing functions. The offences also include impersonation by non-officials and officials, and false representations about Commonwealth entities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant to DTIAs involving Commonwealth public officials and entities, as it specifies conditions under which impersonation or obstruction of officials constitutes an offence, impacting data transfer and processing involving Commonwealth entities.

requirements.text: The person knows that another person is a public official
The person obstructs, hinders, intimidates or resists the official in the performance of the official's functions
The official is a Commonwealth public official
The functions are functions as a Commonwealth public official
The person impersonates another person in that other person's capacity as a Commonwealth public official
The person does so knowing it to be in circumstances when the official is likely to be on duty
The person does so with intent to deceive
The person falsely represents himself or herself to be a Commonwealth public official in a particular capacity
The person does so in the course of doing an act or attending a place in the assumed capacity

sections.source_quote: Section 149.1: (a) the person knows that another person is a public official; (b) the first-mentioned person obstructs, hinders, intimidates or resists the official in the performance of the official's functions; (c) the official is a Commonwealth public official; (d) the functions are functions as a Commonwealth public official.
